keyPoints: Mathematical Model: A simplified mathematical representation of a real-world situation
Modelling Process Step 1: Recognise a real-world problem and state objectives
Modelling Process Step 2: Make assumptions to simplify the problem
Modelling Process Step 3: Formulate a mathematical model based on the variables
Modelling Process Step 4: Use the model to make predictions
Modelling Process Step 5: Collect experimental data and compare with predictions
Modelling Process Step 6: Refine the model if predictions do not match observed data
Advantages of Models: Quick, cheap, easily adaptable, and avoid ethical or practical issues of real experiments
Mean: x̄ = Σx / n for raw data, or x̄ = Σfx / Σf for frequency distributions
Median: The middle value when data is ordered, giving the 50th percentile (Q2)
Mode: The most frequently occurring value or class in the data set
Range: The difference between the highest and lowest values
Lower Quartile (Q1): The value at the 25th percentile of ordered data
Upper Quartile (Q3): The value at the 75th percentile of ordered data
Interquartile Range (IQR): IQR = Q3 - Q1, measuring the spread of the middle 50% of data
Variance (σ²): The mean of the squares minus the square of the mean: σ² = (Σx² / n) - x̄²
Standard Deviation (σ): The square root of variance, measuring average deviation from the mean
Linear Coding: If y = (x - a) / b, then mean ȳ = (x̄ - a) / b and standard deviation σ_y = σ_x / b
Outliers: Extreme values typically defined as > Q3 + 1.5×IQR or < Q1 - 1.5×IQR
Box Plot: A visual diagram showing the minimum, Q1, median, Q3, maximum, and outliers
Histogram: A chart for continuous data where the area of the bar represents frequency
Frequency Density: Used for histogram y-axis, calculated as Frequency / Class Width
Cumulative Frequency: A running total of frequencies, always plotted at the upper class boundary
Symmetrical Distribution: Mean = Median = Mode, and Q3 - Q2 = Q2 - Q1
Positive Skew: Mean > Median > Mode, and Q3 - Q2 > Q2 - Q1 (tail points right)
Negative Skew: Mean < Median < Mode, and Q2 - Q1 > Q3 - Q2 (tail points left)
Stem and Leaf Diagram: Displays the shape of a distribution while retaining original raw data values
Comparing Distributions: Always compare one measure of location (e.g., median) and one measure of spread (e.g., IQR)
Probability Range: For any event A, 0 ≤ P(A) ≤ 1
Complementary Event: The probability of an event not happening is P(A') = 1 - P(A)
Addition Rule: P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A ∩ B)
Mutually Exclusive Events: Events that cannot happen together, where P(A ∩ B) = 0
Independent Events: The occurrence of one event does not affect the other, where P(A ∩ B) = P(A) × P(B)
Conditional Probability: The probability of A given B has occurred is P(A | B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B)
Tree Diagrams: Used for sequential events; multiply probabilities along branches to find intersections
Venn Diagrams: Visual representations of sets used to calculate unions, intersections, and conditional probabilities
Sum of Exhaustive Events: The sum of probabilities of all mutually exclusive and exhaustive outcomes is 1
Scatter Diagram: A graph to visualize the relationship between two variables (x and y)
Explanatory Variable (x): The independent variable plotted on the horizontal axis
Response Variable (y): The dependent variable plotted on the vertical axis
PMCC (r): Product Moment Correlation Coefficient measures linear strength, where -1 ≤ r ≤ 1
Causation: A strong correlation (r close to 1 or -1) does not automatically imply that one variable causes the other
Regression Line of y on x: Equation y = a + bx used to estimate y for a given x
Gradient (b): Represents the predicted change in the response variable y for a 1-unit increase in x
Intercept (a): Represents the predicted value of y when x = 0
Interpolation: Making a prediction within the range of the given data (generally reliable)
Extrapolation: Making a prediction outside the range of the given data (unreliable)
Discrete Random Variable: A variable X that can only take specific, distinct values with associated probabilities
Probability Distribution: A table or formula showing all possible outcomes of X and their probabilities, where Σ P(X=x) = 1
Discrete Uniform Distribution: All possible values of X have an equal probability of occurring
Expected Value (Mean): E(X) = μ = Σ x P(X=x)
Expected Value of a Function: E(g(X)) = Σ g(x) P(X=x)
Variance: Var(X) = σ² = E(X²) - (E(X))²
Linear Transformation of Mean: E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b
Linear Transformation of Variance: Var(aX + b) = a²Var(X)
Cumulative Distribution Function: F(x) = P(X ≤ x), representing the running total of probabilities
Normal Distribution: A continuous, bell-shaped, symmetrical distribution denoted by X ~ N(μ, σ²)
Properties: Symmetrical about the mean, so Mean = Median = Mode, and the total area under the curve is 1
Standard Normal Distribution: Denoted by Z ~ N(0, 1) with mean 0 and variance 1
Standardisation Formula: Converts any normal variable to a standard normal variable using Z = (X - μ) / σ
Using Tables: The statistical tables provide Φ(z) = P(Z < z) for positive values of z
Symmetry Rule: For negative values, use the symmetry property P(Z < -z) = 1 - P(Z < z)
Inverse Normal: Using the probability area to read backwards from the tables to find the corresponding z-value
Percentage Points: Approximately 68% of data lies within μ ± σ, 95% within μ ± 2σ, and 99.7% within μ ± 3σ
Finding Unknown Parameters: Form simultaneous linear equations using Z = (X - μ) / σ to find unknown μ or σ
